title: Walnut pencil box
subtitle: A small pinned-and-glued walnut box with a rebated drop-on lid
excerpt: A simple walnut pencil box 220 mm long, 55 mm wide, and 35 mm deep, with a drop-on lid retained by a narrow rebated shoulder. Glued and pinned at the corners. Walnut's close grain and warm colour make a quiet, handsome object from a small offcut. A first box project in a hardwood.
sourceNotes: Victorian domestic craft manuals for small wooden boxes and stationery cases; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885).

content.text: Keep both hands behind the cutting edge. Secure the workpiece in a vice or with a bench hook before sawing.
Walnut is forgiving to work: the grain is straight in most seasoned boards, the surface planes to a glassy finish without a card scraper, and the dark colour conceals minor glue marks. The
on the lid underside is the only joint requiring accuracy. Everything else is saw-to-the-line and glue-up.

Seasoned walnut. Two side walls, 220 mm by 35 mm by 9 mm. Two end walls, 55 mm by 35 mm by 9 mm. A base, 220 mm by 55 mm by 6 mm. A lid, 224 mm by 59 mm by 9 mm, with a 2 mm shoulder all round on the underside.
Saw the four walls to length, checking square at each end. Mark and saw the base to fit inside the walls.
Plane the long edges of the walls until the face is smooth, working with the
Glue and pin the four walls together, checking square with the combination square. Allow to set, then glue and pin the base.

Cut the lid 2 mm wider and 2 mm longer than the box opening on all sides. Mark the rebate shoulder with a marking gauge: 2 mm down from the underside face, 2 mm in from each edge. Chop and pare the shoulder with a bench chisel, testing the fit in the opening frequently. The lid should drop in under its own weight with a slight friction fit; it should not rattle.

Smooth all outer faces with a block plane and, if available, a card scraper. Walnut benefits from a single coat of oil rubbed in with a cloth, wiped off before it dries. The oil brings out the warm brown of the heartwood without a thick film finish.

items.fix: Test the lid against the opening and feel which corner binds. Mark it with a pencil. Pare only that corner, one stroke at a time, re-testing after each pass.
items.cause: Shoulder pared to an uneven depth, thinning at one corner
items.symptom: Lid shoulder fits at one corner but not others
